did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning model based on transfer learning, specifically DenseNet201 (a CNN architecture), to classify seven types of skin lesions (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermafibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular) from skin images, achieving 99.51% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' (and 'Araştırma Makalesi' in Turkish) in the abstract section. It presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a deep learning model (DenseNet201) using the ISIC 2018 dataset to classify seven types of skin lesions, reporting specific performance metrics such as 99.51% accuracy and F1 scores.

title: Skin Lesions Identification and Analysis with Deep Learning Model Using Transfer Learning
journal: Osmaniye Korkut Ata Üniversitesi Fen Bilimleri Enstitüsü Dergisi
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning model based on transfer learning using the DenseNet201 architecture to classify seven types of skin lesions (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermafibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular) from skin images. The model was trained and tested on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 dataset without any pre-processing. Evaluation was conducted using the KFold3 cross-validation technique, comparing the proposed model against a basic DenseNet201 baseline. The proposed model achieved an average accuracy of 98.73%, with an overall accuracy of 99.51% reported in the abstract, significantly outperforming the basic model's 80.96% accuracy. Key performance metrics included an average F1 score of 98%, recall of 97%, and precision of 98%. The results demonstrate that the proposed transfer learning-based DenseNet201 model provides high-accuracy classification for assisting medical personnel in early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 327 images; described as a deadly type of skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin lesions (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermafibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify skin cancer types' and 'classify seven types of skin lesions'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (classes) from the ISIC 2018 dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, recall, and precision, which are standard metrics for classification tasks. The output is a class label, not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of different skin lesions, widely used in the literature for skin cancer classification.

use.training: Used for training the proposed DenseNet201 transfer learning model and the basic DenseNet201 baseline model.
use.validation: Used for validation via KFold3 cross-validation technique.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed model and baseline model via KFold3 cross-validation technique.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the ISIC 2018 dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate model performance and compare the proposed DenseNet201 model against a basic DenseNet201 baseline.
To determine per-class accuracy rates for the seven skin lesion types and identify specific classification errors.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of average metrics (Accuracy, F1 Score, Recall, Precision)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively compare the performance of the proposed transfer learning model against the basic DenseNet201 model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and the baseline were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, F1 score, recall, precision) and percentage differences without statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.input_size: 32x32x3 (width, height, and volume images with three color channels)

architecture_details.base_architecture: DenseNet201 (CNN architecture developed by Huang et al., 2017)
architecture_details.layer_modifications: Fully connected and classification layers of the basic DenseNet201 were removed. New layers added: maximum pooling (5x5 windows), dropout (0.5 neuron dropout), and flatten layer.
architecture_details.trainable_layers: Layers with moving mean and moving variance properties were set as trainable; others as non-trainable.
architecture_details.internal_structure: Includes transition layers (Transition layer 1: 28x28x128, Transition layer 2: 14x14x256, Transition layer 3: 7x7x896) and Dense blocks (Dense block 1: 56x56x256, Dense block 2: 28x28x512, Dense block 3: 14x14x1792, Dense block 4: 7x7x1920). Dense blocks use batch normalization, ReLU activation, and 3x3 convolution windows.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but loss values were tracked (e.g., average loss 0.38 for proposed model)

classification_or_output_head.output_classes: 7 (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermafibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular)
classification_or_output_head.activation: Softmax (implied by classification task and comparison to other models using softmax)

preprocessing_techniques: No pre-processing was applied to the images; the study explicitly aimed to classify skin lesions without any pre-processing.
Input image resizing to 32x32x3 dimensions (width, height, and volume with three color channels) to increase processing speed.
conclusions: The proposed transfer learning-based DenseNet201 model achieved high accuracy in classifying seven types of skin lesions (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermafibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular) without any image pre-processing.
The proposed model significantly outperformed the basic DenseNet201 baseline, achieving an average accuracy of 98.73% compared to the baseline's 80.96% across KFold3 cross-validation.
The proposed model demonstrated superior performance metrics with an average F1 score of 98%, recall of 97%, and precision of 98%, compared to the baseline's 82%, 79%, and 81% respectively.
The model achieved an overall accuracy of 99.51% as reported in the abstract, and a test accuracy of 97.5% on held-out data.
Per-class analysis showed high accuracy rates, with 100% accuracy for dermafibroma (df) and basal cell carcinoma (bcc), and accuracies ranging from 97.03% to 99.11% for the other classes.

The proposed model's performance (98.73% accuracy) was superior to several comparable studies in the literature using the ISIC 2018 dataset, including MobileNet (83.15%), PNASNet-5 Large (76.0%), and various models by Esteva et al. and Kawahara and Hamarneh.
The study confirms that transfer learning with DenseNet201 is an effective method for assisting medical personnel in the early detection of skin cancer types.

While the model performed satisfactorily, the authors noted that basic CNN methods might be more suitable for real-time hardware systems due to lower computational complexity, despite potentially lower performance compared to deep architectures like DenseNet.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which may introduce dataset bias and limits the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the ISIC 2018 dataset using KFold3 cross-validation, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific dataset.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the proposed model over baselines or literature comparisons.
The input image resolution was reduced to 32x32x3 pixels to increase processing speed, which is unusually low for dermoscopic image analysis and may discard critical diagnostic details.
No data augmentation techniques were applied, which is a significant limitation given the class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset (e.g., nv: 6705 vs. df: 115 images).
The reported accuracy of 99.51% in the abstract differs from the 98.73% average accuracy reported in the results section, creating ambiguity regarding the final performance metric.
The study compares its results against literature values without ensuring identical data splits or preprocessing, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid.
The author declares 100% contribution to the article, indicating a lack of independent verification or collaborative validation.
The paper acknowledges that basic CNN methods might be more suitable for real-time hardware systems due to lower computational complexity, despite the proposed model's higher accuracy.

The study classifies seven skin lesion types but does not address the clinical challenge of distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions within these categories, focusing only on type identification.
